scenarioName: Excited Delirium Progressing to Sudden Cardiac Arrest

scenarioStory.history: 34-year-old male found running in the street with no clothes on a 95°F afternoon, screaming incoherently, sweating profusely. Witnesses report he was "on something" and had been acting strangely for several hours. Police arrived first and have him in handcuffs prone on the asphalt; they describe a prolonged struggle to control him. No reported trauma. Patient is hot to touch, diaphoretic, with dilated pupils and bizarre, paranoid speech.
scenarioStory.course: Initial Contact: Arrival on scene. Patient prone on hot asphalt with two officers maintaining handcuff restraint. Sympathomimetic toxidrome is unmistakable: severely agitated, diaphoretic, hyperthermic, tachycardic, hypertensive, with mydriasis. PD reports an extended struggle. This is a textbook excited delirium / delirium with agitated behavior scenario with extreme risk of sudden cardiopulmonary arrest. Crew must establish scene safety, get the patient off the prone position, complete a rapid focused assessment, and prepare for both chemical restraint and aggressive cooling. → Chemical restraint + cooling: Patient repositioned supine with soft restraint to stretcher, all four extremities individually secured, no chest-wall restriction. Ketamine 4 mg/kg IM (or midazolam 5 mg IM) administered for chemical restraint; clothing removed; ice packs placed to neck, axillae, and groin; ambulance compartment pre-cooled. Cardiac monitor, SpO2, and capnography in place. Over the next 3–5 minutes the sympathomimetic surge starts to wane: heart rate and BP drift down, patient sedates to GCS 11, but he is still hyperthermic and acidotic from the prolonged struggle. The team must NOT relax — this is exactly when delirium-with-agitated-behavior patients arrest. → Sudden PEA arrest: Patient abruptly loses pulse on the stretcher. Monitor shows organized but widening QRS at 70, progressing to PEA. EtCO2 drops from 32 to 12. This is the moment. The entire scenario pivots: the team must clear restraints, expose the chest, initiate compressions, and run an ACLS algorithm tailored to this patient's reversible causes — hyperthermia (continue cooling), acidosis from prolonged struggle (sodium bicarb), and sodium-channel toxicity if QRS wide (sodium bicarb). Epinephrine 1 mg IV early (PEA = non-shockable, prioritize epi). NO defibrillation indication unless rhythm changes. → Fragile ROSC: Pulse returns at 88 sinus tach. BP 86/52, EtCO2 climbing to 28, but patient remains deeply comatose (GCS 4), core temp still 102.4°F, and the team is at maximum risk of complacency. Re-arrest is common in this exact window. Goals: secure airway, avoid hyperventilation, maintain SpO2 94–98%, support BP, KEEP COOLING, serial 12-leads (rule out STEMI / wide QRS), transport to closest appropriate facility with cath/critical-care capability. Mechanical CPR or hands-ready posture maintained against re-arrest.

patientInformation.patientCondition: Severe agitation, naked in street, suspected stimulant intoxication

scenarioEvents.name: Initial Contact
scenarioEvents.description: Arrival on scene. Patient prone on hot asphalt with two officers maintaining handcuff restraint. Sympathomimetic toxidrome is unmistakable: severely agitated, diaphoretic, hyperthermic, tachycardic, hypertensive, with mydriasis. PD reports an extended struggle. This is a textbook excited delirium / delirium with agitated behavior scenario with extreme risk of sudden cardiopulmonary arrest. Crew must establish scene safety, get the patient off the prone position, complete a rapid focused assessment, and prepare for both chemical restraint and aggressive cooling.
relatedChecklist: Confirm PD scene control; do not enter until secure; coordinate roles
Get patient OFF prone position immediately; never hog-tie or weight on chest
Rapid ABCs; check glucose and temp; rule out head injury / hypoglycemia
Recognize delirium-with-agitated-behavior — imminent arrest risk; verbalize to crew
Apply cardiac monitor, SpO2, and capnography NOW — before sedation

scenarioEvents.name: Chemical restraint + cooling
scenarioEvents.description: Patient repositioned supine with soft restraint to stretcher, all four extremities individually secured, no chest-wall restriction. Ketamine 4 mg/kg IM (or midazolam 5 mg IM) administered for chemical restraint; clothing removed; ice packs placed to neck, axillae, and groin; ambulance compartment pre-cooled. Cardiac monitor, SpO2, and capnography in place. Over the next 3–5 minutes the sympathomimetic surge starts to wane: heart rate and BP drift down, patient sedates to GCS 11, but he is still hyperthermic and acidotic from the prolonged struggle. The team must NOT relax — this is exactly when delirium-with-agitated-behavior patients arrest.
relatedChecklist: Ketamine 360 mg IM (90 kg × 4 mg/kg = 360 mg); 3–5 min onset
Alternative: Midazolam 5 mg IM (10–15 min onset)
Supine; soft restraints x4 to stretcher frame; never restrict chest wall
Active cooling: strip clothes, ice packs to neck/axillae/groin, cool NS bolus
IV access; 1 L cool NS bolus (90 kg × 20 mL/kg = 1800 mL; start with 1 L)
Continuous ECG, SpO2, EtCO2 — watch for hypoventilation, QRS widening, arrhythmia
12-lead EKG; watch for wide QRS (cocaine → Na-channel block)

scenarioEvents.name: Sudden PEA arrest
scenarioEvents.description: Patient abruptly loses pulse on the stretcher. Monitor shows organized but widening QRS at 70, progressing to PEA. EtCO2 drops from 32 to 12. This is the moment. The entire scenario pivots: the team must clear restraints, expose the chest, initiate compressions, and run an ACLS algorithm tailored to this patient's reversible causes — hyperthermia (continue cooling), acidosis from prolonged struggle (sodium bicarb), and sodium-channel toxicity if QRS wide (sodium bicarb). Epinephrine 1 mg IV early (PEA = non-shockable, prioritize epi). NO defibrillation indication unless rhythm changes.
relatedChecklist: Clear restraints from chest/arms; high-quality CPR; pads on
Epinephrine 1 mg IV/IO (1:10,000) q3–5 min; PRIORITIZE early in PEA
Sodium bicarbonate 90 mEq IV (90 kg × 1 mEq/kg = 90 mEq) for acidosis / wide QRS
CONTINUE active cooling during arrest — ice packs, exposed chest, cool NS
Cool NS 2 L wide-open (Hs: hypovolemia from prolonged exertion/diaphoresis)
Use EtCO2 to gauge compression quality (target >10–20 mmHg; rising = good)
BVM 10/min with OPA; advanced airway deferred until 3–4 cycles done
DO NOT defibrillate — PEA is non-shockable; reassess rhythm q2 min

scenarioEvents.name: Fragile ROSC
scenarioEvents.description: Pulse returns at 88 sinus tach. BP 86/52, EtCO2 climbing to 28, but patient remains deeply comatose (GCS 4), core temp still 102.4°F, and the team is at maximum risk of complacency. Re-arrest is common in this exact window. Goals: secure airway, avoid hyperventilation, maintain SpO2 94–98%, support BP, KEEP COOLING, serial 12-leads (rule out STEMI / wide QRS), transport to closest appropriate facility with cath/critical-care capability. Mechanical CPR or hands-ready posture maintained against re-arrest.
relatedChecklist: BVM 8–10/min; target EtCO2 35–45; do NOT hyperventilate
Titrate FiO2 to SpO2 94–98% — do not hyperoxygenate
Continue active cooling until core temp <102.2°F — patient is 102.4°F
Serial 12-lead EKG — assess for STEMI, persistent wide QRS
Continue cool NS bolus; reassess BP q3 min; prep push-dose epi if SBP <90
Anticipate re-arrest; pads on, defibrillator armed, mechanical CPR ready
Transport to closest appropriate center (cath-capable / post-arrest care)

scenarioEvents.name: No monitor after sedation
scenarioEvents.description: Crew administered ketamine 4 mg/kg IM and then turned to paperwork and equipment, leaving the patient under PD watch on the lawn without the cardiac monitor, SpO2, or capnography in place. Approximately 4 minutes later, an officer notices the patient is no longer breathing and is unresponsive — the arrest is unwitnessed by EMS. By the time monitor is finally applied, rhythm is fine VF transitioning to asystole, downtime is uncertain, and EtCO2 is <10. Outcome is poor; resuscitation should still be attempted but the lost monitoring window cost the patient.
relatedChecklist: Recognize lesson: monitor BEFORE / WITH sedation, never after
Initiate late CPR; defib VF; epi q3–5 min; outcome likely poor

scenarioEvents.name: Hot transport, no cooling
scenarioEvents.description: Patient was sedated with ketamine 4 mg/kg IM and loaded into a warm ambulance compartment still wearing his clothing, with no ice packs and no compartment cooling. Crew is monitoring (ECG/SpO2/EtCO2) but has deferred active cooling. En route, core temperature climbs from 104.0°F to 105.4°F; rhythm degrades to wide-complex sinus tach with PVCs, then to wide-complex PEA. Resuscitation is started but the persistent hyperthermia + acidosis make ROSC unlikely without aggressive in-the-back cooling that should have been initiated on scene.
relatedChecklist: Recognize lesson: active cooling starts ON SCENE, not after arrest
Sodium bicarb 90 mEq IV (1 mEq/kg) for wide-QRS + lactic acidosis
Strip patient now; ice packs; cool NS 2 L wide-open; max AC
